mnemonics.title: RMI Components
mnemonics.content: RMI has 4 main components: Remote Interface (defines methods), Remote Implementation (implements interface), Stub (client proxy), Skeleton (server proxy). Think: R.I.R.I.S - Remote Interface, Remote Implementation, Stub, Skeleton.

mnemonics.title: RMI Steps
mnemonics.content: RMI process flow: 1) Create Remote Interface, 2) Implement Remote Class, 3) Create Stub/Skeleton using rmic, 4) Start Registry, 5) Register Object, 6) Client Lookup. Memory: I I S R R L (IImplement, IStart, SRegister, RRegister, LLookup).

mnemonics.title: Remote Method Rules
mnemonics.content: All remote methods must: 1) Throw RemoteException, 2) Use only serializable parameters, 3) Be declared in Remote interface. Think: TES - Throw exception, Everything serializable.

acronyms.usage: Naming service for locating remote objects
Tool to generate stub and skeleton classes
Checked exception for RMI communication failures
Middleware for object communication (used in CORBA, not RMI)

visualTips.visualization: Draw two JVM boxes side by side. Show Client → Stub → Network → Skeleton → Server flow with arrows. Label arrows with 'marshall request' and 'unmarshall response'.
Draw a 3-layer pyramid: Top=Application (your code), Middle=Stub/Skeleton layer, Bottom=Transport layer (TCP/IP). Show data flow through each layer.

Draw a simple diagram showing Client JVM on left, Server JVM on right. Show arrows for 'pass-by-value' for serializable objects with objects inside boxes being 'serialized' (converted to dotted lines).

analogies.analogy: Think of a stub like a restaurant waiter taking your order to the kitchen and bringing back your food - you interact with the waiter (stub), not directly with the chef (remote object).
The RMI Registry is like a phone book or directory - you look up a name (object) to get its contact information (reference) to communicate with it.
Serialization is like packing a suitcase for travel - you convert your belongings (objects) into a portable format (byte stream) to send across (network).
Distributed objects are like a franchise restaurant - the brand (interface) looks the same everywhere, but the actual food is prepared at different locations (JVMs).
